attrs.alt: User Access Administrator — can manage RBAC and locks, cannot modify resources

content.text: User Access Administrator (UAA) is the built-in role dedicated to access management. Its Actions include Microsoft.Authorization/* (full control over role assignments, role definitions, and resource locks) and */read (view all resources at the scope). It grants no resource write operations — a UAA holder can see everything and control who has access, but cannot create, modify, or delete any Azure resource.

This separation makes UAA valuable in two scenarios: a dedicated RBAC management team that controls access without touching resources, and IaC pipelines that need to assign roles to managed identities during deployment without being granted the broader Owner role.

# Inspect the User Access Administrator role definition
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'User Access Administrator' | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

# Key fields:
# Id         : 18d7d88d-d35e-4fb5-a5c3-7773c20a72d9
# Actions    : [
#   '*/read',                    <- view all resources
#   'Microsoft.Authorization/*', <- full access mgmt (create/delete/read role assignments)
#   'Microsoft.Support/*'        <- create and manage support tickets
# ]
# NotActions : []               <- no exclusions
# DataActions: []               <- no data plane

# What 'Microsoft.Authorization/*' covers:
# Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write  (create role assignments)
# Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/delete (remove role assignments)
# Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/read   (view assignments)
# Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/write  (create custom roles)
# Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/delete (delete custom roles)
# Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write            (create resource locks)
# Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete           (remove resource locks)

# Note: UAA cannot grant itself Owner — it can only grant roles it holds itself
# (Azure RBAC elevation principle). A UAA holder cannot grant Owner
# unless they are also Owner at that scope.

UAA + Contributor at the same scope is the functional equivalent of Owner, minus the elevateAccess operation. This combination is useful when you want to be explicit about granting two distinct capabilities rather than using Owner as a shortcut.

A UAA holder can create, modify, and delete role assignments and custom role definitions at their assigned scope. An important RBAC principle applies: a UAA holder can only grant roles that they themselves hold at that scope. They cannot escalate privileges by granting a role they do not possess — this prevents UAA from being used to self-escalate to Owner.

# Common UAA tasks: managing role assignments

# UAA can assign ANY role at or below their UAA scope
# But they can only grant roles that they themselves hold (anti-elevation)

# Example: UAA + Reader at subscription scope
# -> Can assign Reader, Contributor, Owner, or any role to others
# -> Wait — can they grant Owner? Only if they themselves have Owner at that scope.

# Example: UAA (only) at subscription scope — no other role
# -> Can grant Reader (they have */read from UAA's definition)
# -> Can grant roles they hold (UAA itself) but NOT Owner or Contributor
# (Because they don't hold those roles, they cannot grant them)

# Assign roles as a UAA holder
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<new-team-member-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'

# Remove a role assignment
Remove-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<departing-user-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'

# Manage custom role definitions
$newRole = @{
    Name = "App Service Deployer"
    IsCustom = $true
    Description = "Deploy App Service only"
    Actions = @("Microsoft.Web/sites/*", "Microsoft.Web/serverfarms/read")
    NotActions = @()
    DataActions = @()
    NotDataActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @("/subscriptions/<sub-id>")
}
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $newRole

# Create a resource lock
New-AzResourceLock -LockName 'prod-rg-delete-lock' `
    -LockLevel CanNotDelete `
    -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' `
    -Force

UAA appears in three practical scenarios: dedicated RBAC management teams, pipeline service principals that create role assignments during deployment, and team leads who manage access for their own resource groups.

# Pattern 1: UAA + Contributor = effective Owner (minus elevate access)
# Use when a team needs to both manage resources AND manage access to those resources
$teamGroup = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'Team Alpha'"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/alpha-rg'
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/alpha-rg'

# Pattern 2: UAA-only for a dedicated RBAC management team
# The team can manage who has access but cannot modify the resources themselves
$accessTeam = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'RBAC Admins'"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $accessTeam.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

# Pattern 3: UAA at resource scope for managed identity setup
# The deploying service principal needs UAA at the Key Vault scope to assign KV roles to MIs
$deploySp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "DisplayName eq 'deploy-pipeline'"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $deploySp.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/prod-kv' `
    -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal

# This lets the deploy pipeline assign 'Key Vault Secrets User' to the app's managed identity
# during deployment, without needing Contributor or Owner on the vault
Pattern 1 (UAA + Contributor = effective Owner): appropriate for application team leads who own both the resources and the access model for their resource group.
Pattern 2 (UAA only): a centralized access governance team that reviews and executes all role assignment changes but never modifies resources.
Pattern 3 (UAA at resource scope): a deployment pipeline that assigns roles to managed identities it creates, scoped to the specific resources — not the whole subscription.
UAA in Infrastructure as Code Pipelines

The most common reason to grant UAA to a service principal is to allow IaC pipelines to include azurerm_role_assignment (Terraform) or Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments (Bicep/ARM) resources in their deployments. Granting Owner to a pipeline just for this capability is over-privileged. UAA + Contributor gives the pipeline exactly what it needs: the ability to create resources and assign roles to those resources' managed identities.

# IaC scenario: a Terraform pipeline that also assigns roles
# The pipeline service principal needs UAA to call azurerm_role_assignment resources

# Grant Contributor (for resource creation) + UAA (for role assignments) to the pipeline SP
$tfSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "DisplayName eq 'terraform-apply'"
$rgScope = '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/managed-rg'

New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $tfSp.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' -Scope $rgScope `
    -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $tfSp.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' -Scope $rgScope `
    -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal

# Bicep: UAA role assignment for a deployment pipeline
# targetScope = 'resourceGroup'
# var uaaRoleId = '18d7d88d-d35e-4fb5-a5c3-7773c20a72d9'
# resource pipelineUAA 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
#   name: guid(resourceGroup().id, pipelinePrincipalId, uaaRoleId)
#   properties: {
#     roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', uaaRoleId)
#     principalId: pipelinePrincipalId
#     principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
#   }
# }

# Anti-pattern to avoid: granting Owner to a pipeline SP just so it can assign roles
# Owner is far broader than needed. UAA + Contributor is the right separation:
# - Contributor: create/modify/delete resources in the deployment
# - UAA: assign roles to managed identities the deployment creates

Standing UAA at subscription scope is a high-risk configuration — anyone with the assignment can reassign roles at will without any approval. Use PIM to make UAA eligible rather than permanently active. Configure approval requirements so that a senior RBAC administrator reviews and approves activations before they take effect.

# PIM: make UAA eligible rather than permanently active for human administrators
# Access management is a privileged operation — activate it just-in-time

$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'rbac-admin@contoso.com'").Id

# Make UAA eligible at subscription scope (no standing access, must activate)
$params = @{
    action = 'adminAssign'
    justification = 'RBAC admin for platform subscription'
    roleDefinitionId = '18d7d88d-d35e-4fb5-a5c3-7773c20a72d9'  # UAA
    directoryScopeId = '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'
    principalId = $userId
    scheduleInfo = @{
        startDateTime = (Get-Date -Format 'o')
        expiration = @{ type = 'NoExpiration' }
    }
}
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignmentScheduleRequest -BodyParameter $params

# Configure PIM settings to require approval for UAA activation
# (Done in portal: PIM > Azure resources > <subscription> > Roles > User Access Administrator)
# Settings to configure:
# - Maximum activation duration: 8 hours
# - Require MFA on activation: Yes
# - Require justification: Yes
# - Require approval: Yes (approvers = security team lead)

# Audit all UAA activations in the last 7 days
Get-AzRoleAssignmentScheduleInstance -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>' |
    Where-Object {
        $_.RoleDefinitionId -match '18d7d88d' -and
        $_.AssignmentType -eq 'Activated'
    } | Select-Object PrincipalName, StartDateTime, EndDateTime

Because UAA holders can create or delete any role assignment within their scope, auditing who has the role — and what they do with it — is a critical security control. Export UAA assignments across all subscriptions and alert on high-risk assignments at broad scopes.

# Audit all UAA assignments across subscriptions
$subs = Get-AzSubscription
foreach ($sub in $subs) {
    Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId $sub.Id | Out-Null
    $uaaRoleId = 'cab48a64-3512-4852-3521-f7a6b6c8c9d0' # User Access Administrator
    $assignments = Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionId $uaaRoleId
    foreach ($a in $assignments) {
        [PSCustomObject]@{
            Subscription = $sub.Name
            PrincipalName = $a.DisplayName
            PrincipalType = $a.ObjectType
            Scope = $a.Scope
            AssignedAt = $a.RoleAssignmentCreateTime
        }
    }
} | Export-Csv -Path 'uaa-audit.csv' -NoTypeInformation

# Detect dangerous UAA assignments at root or subscription scope
$dangerous = Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object {
    $_.RoleDefinitionName -eq 'User Access Administrator' -and
    ($_.Scope -eq '/' -or $_.Scope -match '^/subscriptions/[^/]+$')
}
$dangerous | Select-Object DisplayName, Scope, ObjectType

# Log Analytics: query UAA role assignment changes
@"
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write'
| where Caller contains 'ServicePrincipal'
| extend principalId = tostring(parse_json(Properties).requestbody)
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, ResourceGroup, ResourceId, Level
| order by TimeGenerated desc
"@ | Invoke-AzOperationalInsightsQuery -WorkspaceId $workspaceId
New member added to UAA group

Users often confuse User Access Administrator with Privileged Role Administrator (PRA). They operate on different planes: UAA governs Azure resource RBAC (ARM) while PRA governs Microsoft Entra ID directory roles. Use both together for hybrid administrative models.

# Privileged Role Administrator (PRA) vs User Access Administrator (UAA)

# PRA lives in Entra ID (Microsoft Graph) — manages DIRECTORY roles
# UAA lives in Azure RBAC (ARM) — manages RESOURCE roles

# Check if current user has PRA role in Entra ID
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagement.Read.Directory'
$praRoleId = '624e87e7-ee1d-4edd-9f56-40edfc5f4c9a'
$praAssignments = Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment -Filter "roleDefinitionId eq '$praRoleId'"
$praAssignments | Select-Object PrincipalId, DirectoryScopeId

# Check if current user has UAA role in Azure RBAC
$uaaAssignments = Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator'
$uaaAssignments | Select-Object DisplayName, Scope, ObjectType

# Scenario: Hybrid admin needing both planes
# Assign PRA (via PIM in Entra) for group/app role management
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleEligibilityScheduleRequest -BodyParameter @{
    action = 'adminAssign'
    roleDefinitionId = $praRoleId
    principalId = 'HYBRID-ADMIN-OBJECT-ID'
    directoryScopeId = '/'
    scheduleInfo = @{ expiration = @{ type = 'afterDuration'; duration = 'P180D' } }
}
# Assign UAA (via PIM in Azure) for resource RBAC management
New-AzRoleEligibleScheduleRequest -Name (New-Guid) -Scope '/subscriptions/SUBSCRIPTION-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionId 'cab48a64-3512-4852-3521-f7a6b6c8c9d0' \`
    -PrincipalId 'HYBRID-ADMIN-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RequestType 'AdminAssign' \`
    -ScheduleInfoExpirationType 'AfterDuration' \`
    -ScheduleInfoExpirationDuration 'P180D'
High — can escalate to Global Admin
Scoped Delegation: Narrowing UAA with Custom Roles

Full User Access Administrator at subscription scope is broad. When a team only needs to assign a fixed set of low-privilege roles within one resource group, create a custom role that mimics UAA but is restricted to that scope and cannot be used to assign Owner or Contributor.

# Narrow UAA to only assign specific roles (scoped delegation pattern)
# This custom role can assign Reader and Monitoring Reader but nothing else
$customRole = @{
    Name = 'Scoped Access Delegator'
    Description = 'Can assign Reader and Monitoring Reader within a resource group'
    Actions = @(
        'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write'
        'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/delete'
        '*/read'
    )
    NotActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @('/subscriptions/SUBSCRIPTION-ID/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring')
}

# Hard-limit: cannot restrict WHICH roles a UAA holder assigns — that
# control lives in Deny Assignments, not role definitions.
# The pattern above creates a weaker custom role that looks like UAA
# but applies only inside one resource group.

New-AzRoleDefinition -Role ([Microsoft.Azure.Commands.Resources.Models.Authorization.PSRoleDefinition](New-Object Microsoft.Azure.Commands.Resources.Models.Authorization.PSRoleDefinition -Property $customRole))

# Assign the scoped delegator instead of full UAA
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'TEAM-LEAD-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Scoped Access Delegator' \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-monitoring'

Least-privilege tip: prefer a custom 'Scoped Access Delegator' over full UAA wherever the assignment task is repetitive and bounded. Reserve UAA for platform engineers who genuinely need flexibility across all roles and scopes.

Azure Lighthouse lets managed service providers (MSPs) operate inside customer tenants through delegated authorization. Critically, UAA cannot be delegated via Lighthouse — Microsoft blocks it to prevent MSPs from escalating privileges by reassigning roles within a customer environment.

# Register Azure Lighthouse delegation from managing tenant
# managing-tenant.json
@{
    '\$schema' = 'https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2018-05-01/subscriptionDeploymentTemplate.json'
    contentVersion = '1.0.0.0'
    parameters = @{ mspName = @{ type='string' }; managedByTenantId = @{ type='string' } }
    resources = @(@{
        type = 'Microsoft.ManagedServices/registrationDefinitions'
        apiVersion = '2020-02-01-preview'
        name = '[guid(parameters(mspName))]'
        properties = @{
            registrationDefinitionName = '[parameters(mspName)]'
            managedByTenantId = '[parameters(managedByTenantId)]'
            authorizations = @(
                # Note: UAA CANNOT be delegated directly via Lighthouse
                # Only specific built-in roles with allowedRoleDefinitionIds are permitted
                @{ principalId = 'SP-OBJECT-ID'; roleDefinitionId = 'acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7' } # Reader
                @{ principalId = 'SP-OBJECT-ID'; roleDefinitionId = 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c' } # Contributor
            )
        }
    })
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10

# Why UAA is blocked in Lighthouse:
# Delegating UAA to a managing tenant would let the MSP re-assign any
# role within the customer tenant — a privilege escalation risk Microsoft
# mitigates by blocking UAA from the Lighthouse role allowlist.
# Use JIT access via PIM or a dedicated break-glass account instead.
Lighthouse allowlist of delegatable roles excludes UAA and Owner.
MSPs requiring access-management capabilities must use JIT access through the customer's own PIM.
Alternative: grant MSP service principal Contributor at resource scope, and keep access management under customer control.
This restriction is by design — enforces a trust boundary between managing and managed tenants.
User Access Administrator is the role for controlling access without controlling resources:
Actions: ['*/read', 'Microsoft.Authorization/*', 'Microsoft.Support/*'] — see everything, manage all RBAC.
No resource write operations — cannot create, modify, or delete Azure resources.
Can create and delete role assignments, create and delete custom role definitions, and manage resource locks.
Cannot grant roles it does not itself hold — no self-escalation to Owner.
UAA + Contributor at the same scope = functional equivalent of Owner (without elevateAccess).
Use PIM with approval requirements for human UAA holders at subscription scope.
IaC pipelines that create role assignments should receive Contributor + UAA at their deployment scope, not Owner.
